title: AI Salary Disparities
AI Salary Disparities
text:
● Lightcast US · 29,798 postings · May–Sep 2024
    Salary disparities acrossdisciplines — who benefitsmost from AI's rise?
    
      A data-driven look at how the AI boom is reshaping pay across skills, experience levels, and geographies.
      Three interactive views, drawn from nearly 30,000 US job postings, reveal where the AI premium is real,
      where it's hype, and where it inverts entirely.
    
    
      
        Source
        Lightcast US
      
      
        Postings
        29,798
      
      
        Period
        May – Sep 2024
      
      
        Authors
        Hasnat & Bakdaulet
      
    
  




  
    
      Authored by
      
        Hasnat
        Bakdaulet
      
    
    Boston University · MS Applied Business Analytics
  




  ▸ Three views, one question
  Where AI actually pays — and where it doesn't
  
    The "AI premium" is real, but it's not evenly distributed. Some skills, career stages, and states see
    double-digit pay bumps; others see the opposite. Each chart below is interactive — hover for exact figures.
  



 

    
      01
      
        Software-skill analysis
        Java leads the listed software skills, with SQL split sharply by label
      
    

    
      
        Java postings show the highest average salary in this table at $137.2K, followed by Sql at
        $123.8K and .NET at $121.3K. Python sits near the middle of the group, while
        the uppercase SQL category is the lowest at $103.8K.
      
      
        The gap suggests skill labels are picking up different role mixes, not just different tools. Backend and
        platform-oriented software skills cluster higher, while broader database-language postings appear more mixed
        in seniority and function.
      
    

    
      Average posted salary by software skill
      Lightcast US job postings, May-Sep 2024
      
        
      
      
        Salaries are averages from the provided table. Categories are kept exactly as supplied, including separate Sql and SQL labels.
      
    
  

  
    
      02
      
        Remote-work analysis
        Remote roles edge out onsite roles by roughly $15.8K on average
      
    

    
      
        Fully remote postings average $116.5K, slightly ahead of postings with no remote designation
        and hybrid remote roles. Not remote postings average $100.7K, making them the clear low point
        in this table.
      
      
        The result lines up with the idea that remote-friendly roles are often more specialized or compete in broader
        labor markets, while onsite roles can reflect more local pay bands and a wider range of job families.
      
    

    
      Average posted salary by remote status
      Lightcast US job postings, May-Sep 2024
      
        
      
      
        [None] is retained as a separate category because the posting did not specify a remote-status label in the provided table.
      
    
  

  
    
      03
      
        State salary analysis
        Connecticut, New Jersey, and Arkansas top the salary ranking
      
    

    
      
        The top three states are tightly packed: Connecticut averages $124.9K, New Jersey
        $122.9K, and Arkansas $122.2K. California appears sixth at
        $120.8K, close to Virginia, Vermont, Montana, and Illinois.
      
      
        The spread across the top ten is relatively narrow, about $6.3K from Connecticut to
        Washington, D.C., suggesting the highest-paying geographies are clustered more tightly than the skill-level
        salary gaps.
      
    

    
      Top 10 states by average posted salary
      Lightcast US job postings, May-Sep 2024
      
        
      
      
        Values are average posted salaries from the provided table. Washington, D.C. is shown with its full district label in the tooltip.
      
    
  





  
    
      01
      
        Skill-level analysis
        Generative AI commands a 49% premium — Excel pays 26% less
      
    

    
      
        At the top of the salary ladder sit the skills that build AI systems — Generative AI (+49%),
        Kubernetes (+34%), Docker (+29%), and AWS (+27%). At the bottom sit the tools that consume
        their outputs — Excel (−26%), Power BI (−17%), and Tableau (−16%) all post salaries well below market median.
      
      
        The takeaway: employers are paying steep premiums for AI infrastructure and ML engineering skills,
        while traditional analyst tooling has become commoditized. This is association, not causation — but the gap
        is striking and consistent across thousands of postings.
      
    

    
      Median salary of postings mentioning each skill, vs. postings that don't
      Lightcast US job postings, May–Sep 2024 · n = 29,798 full-time postings with salary
      
        Pays above market
        Pays below market
      
      
        
      
      
        Read as association, not causation: the skill mix on a posting reflects the kind of role being hired for.
        A "−26% Excel" posting is typically a different job from a "+49% Generative AI" posting, not the same job paid less.
      
    
  

  
  
    
      02
      
        Experience-level analysis
        The AI premium is largest for entry-level — and disappears at the top
      
    

    
      
        For entry-level workers (0–2 years), AI postings pay 19.5% more than non-AI peers — a $15K bump
        on a median ~$80K base. The gap shrinks to roughly 10% for mid- and senior-level roles, then collapses to essentially
        zero at lead/principal (9+ years).
      
      
        The pattern suggests AI is currently a ladder for early-career workers, not a multiplier at
        the top. Once you're senior enough, leadership and domain expertise dominate compensation, and the AI label
        no longer adds a separate premium.
      
    

    
      
        Median posted salary by minimum-years-experience bucket · Lightcast US, May–Sep 2024 · n = 23,116 postings with experience specified
      
      
        
      
      
        AI postings command a $15K bump for entry-level roles but the gap closes by senior level and inverts slightly
        at lead/principal — at the top of the ladder, seniority dominates and AI no longer pays a premium.
      
    
  

  
  
    
      03
      
        Geographic analysis
        Where the AI premium is biggest — not always where you'd guess
      
    

    
      
        Among major tech states, Washington (+33%) and California (+20%) show the
        steepest AI premiums — consistent with cloud and AI hyperscalers concentrated there. Smaller-volume states
        like Rhode Island and South Carolina top the chart, but with thin samples (~30 AI postings each), treat as directional.
      
      
        Kentucky inverts the pattern with a −17% premium: its AI postings skew toward lower-paying
        analyst work rather than ML engineering, a reminder that "AI jobs" isn't a monolithic category — composition
        matters as much as label.
      
    

    
      
        AI vs non-AI median salary gap, states with ≥200 postings · Lightcast US, May–Sep 2024
      
      
        
      
      
        Among major tech states, Washington (+33%) and California (+20%) show the steepest premiums — consistent with
        cloud and AI hyperscalers concentrated there. Smaller-volume states like Rhode Island and South Carolina top
        the chart but the AI sample sizes are small (~30–35 postings), so treat as directional. Kentucky inverts:
        its AI postings skew toward lower-paying analyst work.
      
    
  

  
  ▸ At a glance
  The headline numbers

  
    
      +49%
      Generative AI premium — the largest pay gap of any skill in the dataset
    
    
      +19.5%
      AI premium for entry-level workers — drops to near-zero at lead/principal
    
    
      −26%
      Excel salary discount — traditional analyst tools pay below market

title: About
text:
● The Project · The Authors
    About this analysis
    
      This site is a class project for Boston University's MS in Applied Business Analytics, exploring how
      generative AI is reshaping the US labor market through the lens of posted salaries.
    
  



  

    ▸ The motivation
    Why this question matters
    
      Every quarter brings new headlines about AI either creating six-figure jobs or wiping them out. Both stories
      can be true at the same time — and they are. We wanted to see, in actual posted-salary data, where the AI
      premium is real, where it's small, and where it inverts.
    
    
      The three views on the home page are designed to be read together. The skill chart shows the supply-side
      story (which technical skills employers will pay for). The experience chart shows the career-stage story
      (where in your career the AI label adds the most value). The state chart shows the geography story (where
      that premium concentrates).
    

    What we found, in one sentence
    
      The AI premium is real but uneven — it's biggest for entry-level workers learning to build
      AI systems in cloud-heavy tech states, and it shrinks toward zero for senior workers, traditional analyst
      tools, and regions where AI hiring leans toward analyst roles rather than ML engineering.
    

    ▸ The team
    Authors

    

      
        H
        Hasnat
        CO-AUTHOR · DATA ANALYSIS
        
          MS Applied Business Analytics candidate at Boston University. Focused on the data pipeline,
          skill classification, and the methodology behind the AI vs. non-AI cuts.
        
      

      
        B
        Bakdaulet
        CO-AUTHOR · VISUALIZATION
        
          MS Applied Business Analytics candidate at Boston University and Senior Software Developer at Umojo.
          Focused on the chart design, interactivity, and turning the analysis into a publishable site.
        
      

    

    Tools we used
    
      Python — pandas for data wrangling, scipy for the statistical cuts
      Lightcast US — primary data source (29,798 full-time postings, May–Sep 2024)
      Chart.js — interactive visualization layer
      Quarto — site framework, with custom CSS for the design system
      GitHub Pages — hosting and continuous deployment
    

    Citation
    
      If you reference this work, please cite as:
    
    
      Hasnat & Bakdaulet (2026). Salary Disparities Across Disciplines: Who Benefits Most from AI's Rise?
      Boston University, MS Applied Business Analytics.

title: Methodology
text:
● Data & Methods
    How we measured the AI premium
    
      Every figure on this site comes from one source: the Lightcast US job postings dataset for May–September 2024.
      Below is exactly how we cleaned the data, classified "AI" postings, and computed the premiums.
    
  



  

    ▸ At a glance
    Data specifications

    
      
        Source
        Lightcast US
      
      
        Time period
        May – Sep 2024
      
      
        Total postings
        29,798
      
      
        With experience field
        23,116
      
      
        Coverage
        All 50 US states
      
      
        Posting type
        Full-time, with salary
      
    

    1. Defining an "AI posting"
    
      A posting was classified as AI-related if it mentioned at least one skill from a curated list
      of AI/ML technologies — including Generative AI, Machine Learning, TensorFlow / PyTorch, MLOps, NLP, and
      Computer Vision. Postings without any of those tags formed the comparison "non-AI" group.
    
    
      This is intentionally a broad definition: a job that requires "machine learning" experience
      counts as AI even if the title is "Data Scientist." We chose breadth over precision because narrow
      title-matching would miss most of the AI-adjacent labor market.
    

    2. Computing the premium
    
      For every cut (skill, experience bucket, state) we computed:
    
    
      Median posted salary for AI postings in that cut
      Median posted salary for non-AI postings in the same cut
      Premium = (AI median − non-AI median) / non-AI median × 100
    
    
      We used medians (not means) because posted-salary distributions are heavily right-skewed by a few
      executive-level roles. Medians are robust to those outliers and represent the typical posting more honestly.
    

    3. Filters and thresholds
    
      Salary required: postings without a posted salary range were dropped entirely
      Experience cuts: only postings with a minimum-years-experience field (n = 23,116)
      State cuts: only states with ≥200 total postings — smaller states are flagged as directional
      Skill cuts: only skills with ≥30 AI postings to avoid noisy comparisons
    

    4. What this is — and isn't
    
      These are posted salaries, not realized wages. They reflect what employers offer at the door,
      not what workers ultimately earn after negotiation, bonuses, or equity. They also reflect the
      composition of the AI labor market, not a controlled comparison. A skill premium of "+27% for AWS"
      doesn't mean adding AWS to your résumé will raise your salary 27% — it means the kinds of jobs that list AWS
      tend to pay 27% more on average.
    
    
      Treat every figure on this site as association, not causation. The patterns are real and
      consistent across thousands of postings, but they describe what's happening in the market — not what would
      happen if individual workers acquired specific skills.
    

    5. Limitations
    
      Geography: some states have small AI-posting samples. Rhode Island and South Carolina look
          like outliers but may revert with larger samples
      Seniority: "minimum years experience" is what employers ask for, not what hires
          actually have. Real seniority distributions may differ
      Skill overlap: postings often list 5–15 skills; a single skill's premium reflects the
          full bundle on that posting, not the marginal value of that skill alone
      Time window: 5 months of 2024 data. The AI labor market is moving fast; figures may shift
